title: Washington Clears $700M Engine Sale for Turkey's Kaan Fighter as NATO Summit Looms
subhead: The Trump administration formally notified Congress of a $700 million jet engine sale to Turkey, signaling a major thaw in US-Turkey defense ties ahead of the July 7-8 NATO summit in Ankara — while Erdogan's government cracks down on dissent and advances PKK disarmament legislation at home.

sections.heading: US Clears $700M F110 Engine Sale for Turkey's Kaan Fighter
sections.body:
The Trump administration formally notified Congress on June 25 of its intention to sell dozens of General Electric F110 turbofan engines worth more than $700 million to Turkey, according to Reuters and Al-Monitor. The engines are destined for Turkey's indigenous fifth-generation TF Kaan fighter jet, developed by Turkish Aerospace Industries. The sale had been stalled since 2020 under CAATSA sanctions imposed after Turkey's purchase of Russia's S-400 air defense system.

The notification came just one day after Trump, standing alongside NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte in the Oval Office, hinted at a broader F-35 breakthrough. Asked whether Erdogan would receive a 'big gift' at the summit, Trump said: 'He's a strong member of NATO. I'm going to probably do something that's going to make him very happy.' Vice President JD Vance separately confirmed that the Pentagon, under Secretary Pete Hegseth, is reviewing whether Turkey can re-enter the F-35 program — though Vance noted that 'certain things we have to certify have happened… in order to comply with American law,' a reference to the CAATSA requirement that Turkey cease possession of the S-400.

The top Democrat on the House Foreign Affairs Committee, Gregory Meeks (D-NY), accused the administration of skirting Congress, stating it 'bypassed congressional review' without invoking emergency authority or providing a written rationale. Greek City Times reports that bipartisan opposition in Congress is growing, with both Republican and Democratic lawmakers criticizing the proposed sales.

According to Al-Monitor, the engine sale represents 'the lowest-hanging fruit,' in the words of Middle East Institute Turkey program director Gonul Tol. The bigger question — Turkey's return to the F-35 program — remains blocked by CAATSA until Ankara addresses the S-400. One option under discussion: transferring the S-400 systems to the US-controlled section of Incirlik Air Base or to a Turkish military facility abroad, such as in Qatar, Somalia, or Northern Cyprus.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the most significant positive shift in US-Turkey defense relations since Turkey's 2019 ejection from the F-35 program. The engine sale keeps the Kaan program alive; the F-35 question remains the harder legal and political test. Either way, Washington is clearly choosing engagement over punitive distancing ahead of the Ankara summit.

sections.heading: Erdogan Announces Legal Framework for PKK Disarmament
sections.body:
President Erdogan told AK Party lawmakers on June 24 that work is underway on a legal framework to speed up the disbandment of the PKK, and that it would be brought to parliament 'without too much delay.' The announcement signals movement in what had been a stalled peace process since the PKK's May 2025 decision to disarm and disband, which came in response to an appeal from imprisoned PKK leader Abdullah Ocalan.

According to Reuters, Erdogan said: 'At the point we have reached, we are working on a legal framework that will speed up the disbandment of the group.' He also noted that the integration of Syrian Kurdish militants into Syria's state apparatus — a key element of the broader process — 'was also moving along.' Kurdish politicians have accused the government of moving too slowly since Turkey first announced plans for a legal framework last November. The Iran war further stalled progress due to concerns about regional instability.
sections.whyItMatters: A concrete legal framework for PKK disarmament would be the most significant domestic political development in Turkey in decades. The question is whether Erdogan follows through with real legislative action or keeps the process as managed ambiguity — particularly with elections and the NATO summit as backdrop.

sections.heading: Mass Arrests and Press Restrictions Ahead of NATO Summit
sections.body:
Turkish authorities arrested at least 209 people in overnight raids on June 22-23 in Ankara, according to Human Rights Watch. Those detained included political activists, lawyers, an academic, and prominent LGBT rights activist and journalist Yildiz Tar, editor-in-chief of Kaos GL. The Ankara prosecutor's office linked the arrests to 'revolutionary leftist groups' and ISIS, without providing details of specific alleged crimes.

The arrests came hours after the Ankara governor's office banned all public assemblies and demonstrations from June 28 through July 10 — a 13-day window covering the NATO summit. The ban also covers distributing leaflets and displaying banners.

Separately, dozens of Turkish journalists from independent and critical outlets have been denied accreditation to cover the July 7-8 NATO summit, according to journalism groups cited by the Washington Post and Reuters. NATO said it is in discussions with Turkish authorities about the matter, as the host country guides media accreditation decisions.

HRW deputy director Benjamin Ward said: 'The misuse of terrorism laws to conduct mass arrests and silence people in the run-up to a NATO summit flies in the face of the founding values of the alliance.'
sections.whyItMatters: The pre-summit crackdown creates a direct tension between NATO's stated democratic values and the Erdogan government's intolerance of dissent. With world leaders descending on Ankara, Turkey's rights record will be under unusual scrutiny — and the accreditation denial for critical journalists ensures the story won't be easily ignored.

sections.heading: Opposition Mayors Targeted in Fresh Corruption Raids
sections.body:
Turkish authorities detained 24 people on June 25 in a new round of raids targeting opposition-run municipalities, according to Anadolu Agency. Among those detained were two district mayors from Izmir, both members of the CHP. Raids were also carried out in Istanbul and the provinces of Aydin and Adana.

The CHP has come under sustained pressure since its strong performance in the 2024 local elections. According to Yahoo News/DPA, more than 20 elected CHP mayors are now in prison, and some have been removed from office. The highest-profile case remains that of former Istanbul mayor Ekrem Imamoglu, arrested in March 2025 on corruption charges, which triggered mass protests. The CHP calls the proceedings politically motivated, saying the government aims to prevent Imamoglu from running for president in 2028.

HRW also notes that the CHP's leadership was removed by court order, and that at least 21 journalists and media workers are currently imprisoned in Turkey.
sections.whyItMatters: The systematic targeting of CHP mayors — now in multiple cities — signals that the post-2024 local election pressure campaign is escalating, not winding down. With the presidential cycle approaching, the government is methodically removing opposition figures from elected office.

sections.heading: Central Bank Signals No Rush to Cut Rates Before Late July
sections.body:
Central bank governor Fatih Karahan has ruled out resuming weekly repo funding before the next interest rate-setting meeting, signaling no rush to ease monetary policy before late July, according to Bloomberg. Meanwhile, Garanti BBVA CEO Mahmut Akten told reporters he anticipates a rate cut at the next monetary policy committee meeting — though he characterized it as likely to be small.

The tension between the central bank's cautious stance and market expectations for easing reflects the broader challenge: Turkey's economy still needs lower rates to grow, but the post-Iran-war inflation environment — where oil prices spiked and then partially receded — complicates the picture. The US PCE index hitting a 3-year high of 4.1% in May adds further global pressure on central banks to stay tight.
sections.whyItMatters: Karahan's cautious stance means Turkey's rate path remains data-dependent through the summer. Any July cut will likely be modest. The bigger question is whether the inflation shock from the Iran war has truly passed — and whether the central bank can ease without reigniting lira pressure.

sections.heading: Post-Iran-War Regional Rebalancing: Turkey Between Washington and Tel Aviv
sections.body:
According to an analysis published by The Media Line, Turkish analysts see the US-Iran memorandum as a strategic opening for Ankara — reducing immediate risks on its borders while expanding its diplomatic value to Washington. Barin Kayaoglu of the Social Sciences University of Ankara said the deal offers Turkey 'lower energy and shipping risks, a chance to revive trade with Iran, and less pressure to balance between the United States, Iran, and Israel.'

But the analysis also flags a growing concern: Israeli rhetoric casting Turkey as the next major regional adversary after Iran. Kayaoglu noted that while direct military confrontation remains unlikely, the more realistic risk is Israel trying to harden Washington's view of Ankara — particularly around Turkey's role in Syria and its channels to Hamas.

Meanwhile, a separate commentary in MSN argues that the Erdogan-Sisi rapprochement — which the State Department portrays as a 'diplomatic victory' calming the Eastern Mediterranean — actually represents a Turkey-Egypt military axis that Washington should be concerned about. The piece contends the two countries are building defense ties that could shift the regional balance.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is positioning itself as the post-Iran-war regional power broker — but that expanded role creates new friction with Israel and new questions for Washington about whose side Ankara is really on. The NATO summit will be a key test of whether US engagement (via engine sales, possible F-35 movement) can offset those tensions.

sections.heading: NATO Summit: Defense Bank Launch and Spending Debates
sections.body:
The July 7-8 NATO summit in Ankara will feature the official launch of the Defence, Security and Resilience Bank (DSRB), a new multilateral financial institution designed to address insufficient access to financing for defense-industry companies across the alliance. Romania has signed on as a founding member. The project was shepherded by former NATO Deputy Secretary General Mircea Geoana, who described it as 'an additional financing and innovation instrument' alongside DIANA and the NATO Innovation Fund.

NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte said allies will announce 'tens of billions of dollars' in defense-related deals at the summit, according to Reuters. The summit is also expected to debate defense spending targets — a recurring point of tension, particularly with Trump's demand that allies spend 5% of GDP on defense.
sections.whyItMatters: The DSRB launch and the massive defense contract announcements signal that NATO is institutionalizing its rearmament push. For Turkey as host, the summit is a diplomatic showcase — but the concurrent domestic crackdown and journalist exclusions create an awkward contrast with the alliance's founding values.

bottomLine: A consequential day for Turkey's strategic trajectory: Washington formally greenlit the $700 million F110 engine sale for the Kaan fighter — the biggest positive shift in US-Turkey defense ties since the 2019 F-35 ejection — while Trump openly hinted at a broader F-35 resolution. At home, Erdogan moved on two fronts simultaneously: advancing a legal framework for PKK disarmament while intensifying the pre-summit crackdown with 209 arrests and journalist exclusions that have drawn HRW condemnation. The July 7-8 NATO summit in Ankara will be the stage where all these threads converge — US engagement, Turkish domestic repression, alliance spending debates, and the regional recalibration after the Iran war. The central question is whether Washington's warming defense relationship with Ankara comes with any meaningful conditionality on democratic norms.
sources: Reuters, Al-Monitor, Human Rights Watch, Washington Post (AP), Yahoo News/DPA, Bloomberg, The Media Line, Devdiscourse, Greek City Times, The War Zone, Bursa.ro
